retrieved_docs.title: MacConkey Medium -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: MacConkey agar (MAC) is a bacterial culture medium named after bacteriologist Alfred T. MacConkey (1861-1931). MacConkey agar is a selective and differentiating agar that only grows gram-negative bacterial species; it can further differentiate the gram-negative organisms based on their lactose metabolism. [1] The selective and differentiating properties of MacConkey agar enable utilization for both research and clinical applications. The fermentation of lactose produces organic acids, particularly lactic acid, which decreases the pH of the agar. MAC contains a pH indicator that turns pink under acidic conditions. Therefore, lactose-fermenting-gram-negatives (lactose-fermenters) will form pink colonies, while non-lactose fermenters will form off-white opaque colonies. Even within lactose-fermenters, species will show a varying rate of growth. The rate of growth is also a way to further differentiate organisms in the MAC medium. Lastly, some species that form a capsule appear differently. Altogether, MacConkey agar only grows gram-negative bacteria, and those bacteria will appear differently based on their lactose fermenting ability as well as the rate of fermentation and the presence of a capsule or not. This makes MAC a powerful tool for differentiating and isolating bacterial species from the sample source. MAC is one of the many bacterial cultures clinical microbiologists utilize for diagnostic testing; this is still widely used in the clinical laboratory to identify causal agents from a patient (ie, stool sample).

retrieved_docs.title: MacConkey Medium -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: A pure bacterial culture remains crucial to assess its virulence, its susceptibility to antibiotic therapy, and its genome sequence in order to facilitate the understanding and treatment of caused diseases. MAC's selective and differentiating properties as a culture medium allow isolating colonies of pure bacterial culture from a source sample. Typically in a clinical setting, the collected sample is placed on a panel of many growth media for identification and isolation of bacterial cultures. [3] MAC is one of the widely used growth media as it can work to selectively grow gram-negative bacteria and further differentiate them based on their fermentation profile. Many pathogenic gram-negative bacteria can be differentiated by MAC, especially bacterial gastroenteritis-causing species. The diagnostic potential is immense. MAC is essentially a versatile foundation, in which additional substrates, such as sorbitol, can be incorporated for further differentiation. Another example is the addition of antibiotics for testing drug resistance. In intensive care units, testing multi-drug resistance in gram-negative bacteria is an important surveillance measure. [4] Gram-negative enteric bacteria are a common cause of bacterial gastroenteritis, which is characterized by diarrhea, vomiting, and abdominal cramping. Escherichia coli and Campylobacter jejuni are some of the common causes of bacterial gastroenteritis. When bacterial gastroenteritis is suspected, the patient's specimen can be sampled and cultured on a panel of bacterial cultures, which includes MacConkey medium. MAC contributes to the identification of the causal agent by providing lactose-fermentation profiles in gram-negative species. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: MacConkey Medium -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Key components of the MacConkey medium include crystal violet dye, bile salts, lactose, and neutral red (pH indicator). Crystal violet dye and bile salts halt the growth of gram-positive bacteria. This allows only gram-negative species to form colonies on MAC agar. [2] MacConkey agar contains the essential nutrients required for microorganism growth. Additional key components include crystal violet dye, bile salts, lactose, and neutral red (a pH indicator). The lactose in the agar is a source of fermentation. Lactose-fermenting microorganisms will produce organic acids, particularly lactic acid, which will lower the pH. Neutral red is a pH indicator that turns from off-white to bright red/pink as the pH drops below 6.8. Different species will yield colonies in varying appearances on a MacConkey medium based on their ability to ferment lactose. This gives McConkey agar its differentiating property.

retrieved_docs.title: MacConkey Medium -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Lactose (Lac) positive (pink colonies): Lactose-fermenting species will grow pink colonies. Lactose fermentation produces acidic byproducts that lower the pH, turning the pH indicator pink. Examples of Lac-positive species: Escherichia coli, Enterobacteria, Klebsiella Lac negative (white colonies) Gram-negative bacterial species will still form colonies, but colonies will have a white appearance as there will be no change in pH in the absence of lactose fermentation. Examples of Lac-negative species: Salmonella, Proteus, Yersinia, Pseudomonas No colonies: Gram-positive bacteria will not form any colonies on MacConkey medium. Slow: Weak lactose fermenters will form colonies slower than the rest. Examples of slow lac fermenters: Serratia, Citrobacter Mucoid: (sticky, wet colonies) Encapsulated bacteria produce capsules using lactose. This gives sticky, wet-appearing colonies. Examples of mucoid colony-forming species: Klebsiella, enterobacter

retrieved_docs.title: Stenotrophomonas Maltophilia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Stenotrophomonas maltophilia easily grows on standard culture media, either as a single isolate or mixed with other culture growths like Pseudomonas aeruginosa. [53] Its culture colonies typically are yellow-green in color on nutrient agar, non-hemolytic with a faint lavender color and an ammonia odor on blood agar, and colorless on MacConkey plates since it is non-lactose fermenting. [18] [17] [21] It also resembles Pseudomonas in having opaque flat surfaces with uneven borders. Selective media were shown to be better in isolating Stenotrophomonas maltophilia from non-sterile samples and usually contain imipenem, vancomycin, amphotericin-B, and mannitol/bromothymol blue as an indicator. [2] [62] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Lens Abscess -- Evaluation -- Culture of Lens Aspirate
retrieved_docs.content: In cases with the anterior capsular breach and cortical disturbance, the lens material can be removed by either lens aspiration by bimanual irrigation and aspiration or extracapsular cataract extraction approach or by manual small incision cataract surgical approach. Some of the lens material should be sent for culture, which can reveal colonies of a microorganism after 72 hours of growth. The most common culture media used are blood agar, chocolate agar, MacConkey agar, and Sabround Dextrose agar incubated in bacteriological indicator for 16 hours overnight at 37 degrees Celsius. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Meningococcal Meningitis(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: CSF fluid should also be sent for Gram staining, standard culture, and polymerase chain reaction (PCR). Gram staining is diagnostic in 85% of patients with meningococcal meningitis. Bacteriologic culture establishes a definitive diagnosis. These should be grown on chocolate blood agar at 35 degrees C. N. meningitidis tends to be positive in around 50% of blood cultures and approximately 75% of cerebrospinal fluid. The cerebrospinal fluid assay can be done with latex agglutination, PCR, rapid dipstick test, and loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP). CSF studies with PCR can also be obtained, although these commonly tend to yield false-negative results. PCR has more advantages than culture for diagnosis of meningococcal infection as it is rapid, can detect different strains of even non-viable bacteria. [30] LAMP is a highly accurate rapid test for the diagnosis of meningococcal infection at an emergency than PCR or culture. [31] [32]

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Yersinia Pseudotuberculosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Yersinia pseudotuberculosis is a gram-negative, coccobacillus that is differentiated by its fermentation of sorbitol and ornithine decarboxylase activity, among other features. The optimum growth occurs on MacConkey medium at 20 to 35 degrees C. Yersinia pseudotuberculosis is aerobic and facultatively anaerobic. It grows slowly on blood and chocolate agar plates. It forms small gray and translucent colonies at 24 to 72 hours. It has a good growth pattern on MacConkey or eosin-methylene blue agar plates but is enhanced noticeably at a temperature lower than 28 degrees C when it becomes motile at 25 degrees C. Yersinia pseudotuberculosis is oxidase-negative, catalase-producing, and urea-splitting, and does not ferment lactose.

retrieved_docs.title: Scrofula (Archived) -- Evaluation -- Mycobacterial Culture
retrieved_docs.content: Definitive diagnosis depends upon the isolation and identification of bacteria from specimens. Specimens inoculated in the egg or agar-based medium (Lowenstein-Jensen) incubated at 37-degree Celcius. Culture is positive in 70% to 80% of cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Urine Culture -- Procedures -- Specimen Preservation and Processing
retrieved_docs.content: Specimens are processed routinely using calibrated loops for plating. This method allows for CFU/mL findings as well as the isolation of colonies for identification and susceptibility testing. Some of the most utilized media are blood agar and MacConkey agar. The temperature of the plates should be kept between 35 to 37 degrees Celcius with a recommended incubation time of 24 to 48 hours. However, Oligella urolytica ,  a slow-growing, gram-negative, and rare UTI-causing organism, has been reported to have an incubation of over 48 hours. [13] Specimens from outpatients do not need to be plated on selective media. However, in hospitalized patients, where enterococci are the second leading cause of UTI, laboratory technicians should consider inoculating urine specimens to a medium that is selective for these gram-positive cocci. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: MacConkey Medium -- Nursing, Allied Health, and Interprofessional Team Interventions
retrieved_docs.content: A pure bacterial culture remains essential for the study of its virulence, its antibiotic susceptibility, and its genome sequence in order to facilitate the understanding and treatment of caused diseases. Bacterial cultures are still widely used laboratory techniques that many physicians rely on to make their final diagnoses. Many healthcare professionals are involved in this process, and proper communication and coordination will enhance patient-centered care, improve outcomes and patient safety, and enhance team performance. Bacterial culture from a patient's specimen is a commonly ordered test that many physicians use to aid their medical decision-making process. Nurses are frequently involved in obtaining and transporting samples from the patient to the laboratory. Clinical microbiologists must be trained with a thorough understanding of the principles to run the tests, interpret the results, and report the findings. Sterile sampling techniques and proper handling of the specimen during handling and transport of samples are required to avoid contamination. Any healthcare professionals handling these patient samples must be adequately trained in order to avoid contamination of the samples or accidental exposure and spread of potential pathogens. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Enterobacter Infections -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The gold standard for diagnosing Enterobacter infections is the utilization of cultures. It is recommended that at least two sets of blood cultures be obtained, one aerobic and one anaerobic bottle. MacConkey agar can be used to determine if the specimen is lactose fermenting. Furthermore, indole testing can be performed to differentiate indole negative Klebsiella and Enterobacter and indole positive E. Coli. Enterobacter spp are motile, in contrast to Klebsiella , which is not motile. Other important laboratory studies include: Gram stain (may be helpful in the rapid determination of gram-negative rods before cultures are available) Complete blood count Complete metabolic panel Urinalysis with culture Relevant imaging in accordance with the particular organ system involved can be helpful in guiding management.

retrieved_docs.title: Escherichia coli Infection -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: E. coli Gram-negative bacilli, non-spore-forming, flagellated, facultatively anaerobic Ferments lactose (grows on MacConkey agar) Catalase positive Oxidase negative ETEC Culture: MacConkey agar, indole producing Molecular diagnosis: LT or ST genes EHEC/STEC Culture O157:H7: Sorbitol-MacConkey agar, indole producing Non-0157:H7: MacConkey agar Molecular diagnosis: NAAT of Stx1 and Stx2 EIEC Culture: MacConkey agar (glucose and xylose), indole producing Molecular: NAAT of lacY EPEC Culture: MacConkey agar, indole producing Molecular diagnosis: pEAF plasmid or BFP factor EAEC Culture: MacConkey agar, indole producing Molecular diagnosis: AggR regulon

retrieved_docs.title: Gram-Negative Bacteria -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Whereas the infections produced by gram-negative are practically indistinguishable from other etiologies, laboratory characterization can define the organism and its antimicrobial susceptibility profile. Thus, the gold-standard examination of these infections is the cultures; however, they have the problem of a delay in the result. Another essential exam for bacterial isolates is the Gram stain; although simple, it can quickly distinguish the course of a drug intervention. Because of their heterogeneity, Enterobacteria depend on various biochemical tests (Indol, Voges-Proskauer, catalase, cytochrome oxidase, urease, motility, citrate, ONPG, Dnase, decarboxylation of lysine, gas production, among others), grown in primary isolation agar. Species such as E. coli , because they are fermenters of strong acids, they produce large quantities of mixed acids, which make the pH of the media present pink colonies. Others, such as non-fermenters, grow in primary isolation media (blood agar and MacConkey) in the OF-oxidation/fermentation medium - grow only in the aerobic environment. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing -- Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Dilution of bacterial suspension (commonly 1:20) for MIC must occur within 15 minutes after making the standard inoculum. Saline can be used as a diluent for a small amount of inoculum to create a concentration of 5 x 10 colony-forming units (CFU)/milliliter. As the inoculum is carefully poured over the panel tray and transferred to the panel prongs, the final concentration is expected to be relatively the same. Before inoculating the bacterial suspension into the growth medium, ensure the inoculum is not excessive. This check is accomplished by pressing the swab on the sides of the bacterial suspension tube before inoculating to the Mueller-Hinton agar plate. Inoculate the Mueller-Hinton agar plate with the swab by starting from the top and carefully swabbing from side to side down to the bottom. This step is performed 3 times after each plate rotation (usually 60°) to cover the whole plate.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium serves as the primary reference for the abbreviated term ct scan abdomen. The ct scan abdomen is clinically associated with تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية (abdominal ultrasound imaging). Both imaging modalities are employed to evaluate abdominal pathology, and their clinical association reflects complementary roles in the diagnostic work‑up.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2519
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a basic imaging modality, and both computerized tomography of brain and chest and computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are considered alternative or mapped imaging studies derived from the routine chest x-ray, indicating that these CT examinations may be employed for more detailed evaluation when the findings from a standard chest x-ray are insufficient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: CT of abdomen and pelvis and Computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium are both classified as “Other CT scan,” indicating that each of these imaging procedures is considered an alternative or supplementary CT examination derived from a broader CT scan category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is a fundamental radiographic examination that utilizes plain X‑ray techniques as its method of acquisition. Building on this basic imaging approach, more advanced studies are conceptually derived from the routine chest x-ray. A computerized tomography of chest is directly mapped from the routine chest x-ray as its primary extension, providing cross‑sectional detail of thoracic structures. Additionally, a computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium represents another, more comprehensive variant that is also mapped from the routine chest x-ray, offering combined neuro‑and thoracic evaluation with enhanced contrast visualization. This hierarchy illustrates how basic plain X‑ray methods underpin increasingly sophisticated CT imaging modalities, guiding clinical assessment and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2416
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging reference for several computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is the primary source from which a dedicated Computerized tomography of chest is mapped, indicating a direct relationship between the standard chest radiograph and the more detailed CT chest study. In addition, the routine chest x-ray is also linked, through other mappings, to more extensive CT examinations that combine multiple anatomical regions: Computerized tomography of brain and chest, Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium. These connections illustrate that the routine chest x-ray not only underpins isolated chest CT imaging but also informs broader, multi‑region CT protocols, both with and without the use of intravenous contrast, highlighting its central role in guiding advanced diagnostic imaging strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1921
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging study that can be directly linked to several more advanced computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is primarily mapped to both “CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis” and “Computerized tomography of chest,” indicating that these comprehensive and focused chest CT scans are the main follow‑up modalities when further anatomic detail is required beyond the plain radiograph. Additionally, routine chest x-ray is associated with other, less primary CT options, including “Computerized tomography of brain and chest” and “Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium.” These alternative mappings reflect situations where clinicians may extend evaluation to include intracranial structures or require contrast enhancement for enhanced tissue characterization. Together, these relationships illustrate how a routine chest x-ray can guide the selection of targeted or extensive CT imaging, supporting diagnostic decision‑making and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium and computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium are both identified as imaging studies that are mapped from a routine chest x‑ray, indicating that these contrast‑enhanced CT examinations represent more extensive diagnostic extensions of the basic chest radiograph.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1938
retrieved_docs.content: CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis, Computerized tomography of chest, and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen (both with and without intravenous contrast medium) are imaging procedures that generate a computerized tomographic image (صورة مقطعية محوسبة). Each of these CT examinations is linked to the routine chest x‑ray as a source reference: the CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis and the Computerized tomography of chest are primarily mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, indicating a direct derivation or extension of the standard chest radiograph, whereas the Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen and its contrast‑enhanced variant are mapped from the routine chest x‑ray in an “other” capacity, reflecting an alternative or supplementary relationship. This mapping underscores the routine chest x‑ray’s role as a foundational imaging study that can be expanded into more detailed cross‑sectional CT assessments, all of which produce the same type of output—a computerized tomographic image (صورة مقطعية محوسبة).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2153
retrieved_docs.content: CT of abdomen and pelvis, CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis, and computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium are all classified under the broader category “Other CT scan,” indicating that each of these specific imaging protocols is mapped from or considered a subtype of the generic “Other CT scan” designation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2927
retrieved_docs.content: CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis and Computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium are specific imaging examinations that are classified under the category “Other CT scan.” The “Other CT scan” category itself is linked to a broader relationship with tomografie, indicating that these particular CT studies are part of the wider field of tomography imaging techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is used as a reference point for several advanced computed tomography (CT) examinations. The CT scan of the chest is primarily mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, indicating it is the main derivative imaging study. In addition, CT examinations that include the brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, as well as CT scans encompassing the brain, chest, and abdomen, are also linked to the routine chest x‑ray through other mappings, reflecting supplementary or alternative relationships. These connections illustrate how a basic chest radiograph can serve as a foundational study for more comprehensive, multi‑region CT imaging, sometimes enhanced with contrast agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2832
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium are both primarily mapped from the computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head, indicating that these extensive imaging examinations are derived from the same fundamental CT head imaging technique, with the latter study incorporating intravenous contrast to enhance visualization.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2131
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a reference point for several more advanced computed tomography examinations. Both Computerized tomography of brain and chest and Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are considered alternative mappings derived from a routine chest x-ray, indicating that these comprehensive CT studies may be ordered when a standard chest x-ray is insufficient. Additionally, Computerized tomography of chest is identified as the primary mapping from a routine chest x-ray, reflecting its role as the most direct CT extension of the basic chest radiograph. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians select appropriate imaging modalities, progressing from a routine chest x-ray to targeted CT imaging of the chest alone or combined brain‑chest assessment, with or without contrast, based on diagnostic needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1678
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is used as a primary reference point for both CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis and for a more limited Computerized tomography of chest, indicating that these computed tomography studies often build upon the initial findings of a standard chest radiograph. In addition, a Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium is linked to routine chest x-ray as an alternative mapping, suggesting that the chest x-ray can also inform more extensive, contrast‑enhanced CT examinations that include the brain, chest and abdomen. These relationships highlight how a routine chest x-ray can serve as a foundational imaging study that guides the selection and interpretation of various CT protocols, ranging from focused chest imaging to comprehensive, contrast‑enhanced scans of multiple body regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2617
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is performed using plain X-ray techniques. More advanced imaging studies are conceptually linked to this basic examination: computerized tomography of chest is primarily mapped from the routine chest x-ray, and computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium is also mapped from the routine chest x-ray as an additional related procedure. These connections illustrate how the simple radiographic method of a routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational reference for increasingly complex CT imaging of the chest, as well as combined brain, chest, and abdominal assessments with contrast enhancement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain and chest and Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are both procedures that are primarily mapped from the computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head, indicating that the head CT protocol serves as the foundational reference for these combined brain and chest imaging studies. The version with intravenous contrast medium incorporates contrast administration to enhance visualization of structures, while both examinations rely on the same underlying CT head imaging principles.
